History of Diplomacy: From Ancient Times to Modern Era
======================================================

By

[Zubeda Anjum Niazi](https://thediplomaticinsight.com/author/zubeda123/)

 - 

January 18, 2023

11798

[Twitter](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=History+of+Diplomacy%3A+From+Ancient+Times+to+Modern+Era&url=https%3A%2F%2Fthediplomaticinsight.com%2Fhistory-of-diplomacy-from-ancient-times-to-modern-era%2F&via=DiplomaticIns "Twitter")[Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fthediplomaticinsight.com%2Fhistory-of-diplomacy-from-ancient-times-to-modern-era%2F "Facebook")[WhatsApp](https://api.whatsapp.com/send?text=History+of+Diplomacy%3A+From+Ancient+Times+to+Modern+Era%20%0A%0A%20https://thediplomaticinsight.com/history-of-diplomacy-from-ancient-times-to-modern-era/ "WhatsApp")[Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https://thediplomaticinsight.com/history-of-diplomacy-from-ancient-times-to-modern-era/&title=History+of+Diplomacy%3A+From+Ancient+Times+to+Modern+Era "Linkedin")[Email](mailto:?subject=History%20of%20Diplomacy:%20From%20Ancient%20Times%20to%20Modern%20Era&body=https://thediplomaticinsight.com/history-of-diplomacy-from-ancient-times-to-modern-era/ "Email")[Print](https://thediplomaticinsight.com/history-of-diplomacy-from-ancient-times-to-modern-era/# "Print")

[](https://thediplomaticinsight.com/history-of-diplomacy-from-ancient-times-to-modern-era/# "More")

Diplomacy has been undoubtedly a critical tool for nations to navigate the intricacies of international relations for centuries. The art of diplomacy has evolved, from ancient civilizations to the modern era, to keep pace with the changing world.

We will delve into the rich history of diplomacy in this blog, tracing its origins as well as its evolution from ancient to contemporary times.

From the early diplomatic practices of Mesopotamia and Egypt to the medieval courts of Europe, and the global diplomacy of the 20th century, we will explore the critical moments and figures that have shaped the way nations presently intercommunicate and interact.

We will also take a look through this journey, at how diplomacy has transformed because of new technologies, global events as well as political ideologies.

**Ancient Diplomacy**
---------------------

Diplomacy can be traced back to ancient civilizations like Egypt and Mesopotamia, where diplomatic treaties and marriages were used to form alliances along with negotiating peace.

![Image 3: Ancient Diplomacy](https://thediplomaticinsight.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/01/2-3.jpg)

Ancient Diplomacy

Along these lines, the city-state of Athens in ancient Greece developed a system of diplomacy known as the “[Megarian decree](https://www.livius.org/articles/concept/megarian-decree/).” The decree sought to economically pressure and isolate other city-states.

The economic sanctions were levied upon Megara c. 432 BC hence becoming one of the first uses of economics as a foreign policy tool.

This was shortly before the outbreak of the Peloponnesian War. Likewise, in Mesopotamia, city-states often used diplomatic marriages to establish alliances and negotiate peace.

For example, the Sumerian king [Lugal-Anne-Mundu of Adab](https://ancientmesopotamia.org/people/lugal-anne-mundu) married the daughter of the king of Kish, forming a powerful alliance between their two city-states.

Similarly, pharaohs in ancient Egypt often used diplomatic marriages to amplify their political power. The [pharaoh Ramses II](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Ramses-II-king-of-Egypt) married many foreign princesses to form alliances with other kingdoms.

Gift-giving was another important form of diplomacy in ancient civilizations. The rulers of Mesopotamia and Egypt exchanged gifts like precious metals, jewelry, and works of art as a sign of goodwill and to build relationships.

Also, diplomatic representatives were also used by ancient civilizations to intercommunicate with other states. For instance, emissaries negotiated peace treaties, establish trade agreements and convey important messages on behalf of their states.

Across the board, diplomacy played a crucial role in ancient civilizations, as it allowed them to maintain stable relations with neighboring states. This practice was essential for their prosperity and survival.

**Middle Ages or Medieval Diplomacy**
-------------------------------------

During the Middle Ages, also known as the Medieval period, diplomacy played a paramount part in the economic and political relations between states.

![Image 4: History of Diplomacy](https://thediplomaticinsight.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/01/MODERN-DIPLOMACY-1024x1024.jpg)

Envoys respected for their knowledge and wisdom and known for their skills in diplomatic negotiations were sent to other states to negotiate treaties, convey important messages, and establish trade agreements.

Also just like ancient diplomacy, medieval diplomacy also involved the use of marriages as a political tool as well as the resolution of conflicts.

**Also read:**[5 Tips for Successful Diplomacy: Strategies for Navigating International Relations](https://thediplomaticinsight.com/5-tips-for-successful-diplomacy-strategies-for-navigating-international-relations/)

In this regard, it is pertinent to note that Islamic history has a rich tradition of diplomacy, dating back to the time of the Prophet Muhammad (ﷺ) in the 7th century.

One of the earliest examples of Islamic diplomacy was the [Treaty of Hudaybiyyah](https://www.britannica.com/event/Pact-of-Al-Hudaybiyah), which was signed between the Muslim community in Medina and the Meccan tribe of Quraysh in 628 AD.

Another important example of diplomacy was the correspondence between the [Muslim Caliph Umar](http://www.worldhistory.org/Umar/) and the [Byzantine Emperor Heraclius](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=f81c9de743aa487eJmltdHM9MTY3MzkxMzYwMCZpZ3VpZD0yMjEzOGMxNi1kOTM5LTY3NDUtMWRmOC05ZTVlZDg2MzY2MzAmaW5zaWQ9NTE3NA&ptn=3&hsh=3&fclid=22138c16-d939-6745-1df8-9e5ed8636630&psq=Byzantine+Emperor+Heraclius&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYnJpdGFubmljYS5jb20vYmlvZ3JhcGh5L0hlcmFjbGl1cy1CeXphbnRpbmUtZW1wZXJvcg&ntb=1).

The correspondence was marked by mutual respect and eventually led to a fair and just treaty between the two empires.

During the medieval period, Muslim rulers would send envoys to other states to negotiate treaties, convey important messages, and establish trade agreements.

In this vein, [Sufism](https://sufism.org/sufism) also played a role in diplomacy where Sufi scholars and leaders were known to act as intermediaries and mediators in conflicts between different Muslim and non-Muslim communities.

On the other hand, the Catholic Church as a mediator in conflicts between European states played an important part in diplomacy, during the Middle Ages.

As the spiritual leaders of the Catholic Church, Popes would often use their religious authority to resolve disputes and broker peace treaties.

![Image 5: 5 Tips for Successful Diplomacy: Strategies for Navigating International Relations](https://thediplomaticinsight.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/01/diplomacy-1024x683.jpg)

One renowned example in this context is the [Peace of God movement](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Peace-of-God). It was initiated by the Catholic Church in the 11th century with the aim to protect non-combatants, like merchants and peasants from brutality during the war.

The Church declared that any harm inflicted upon non-combatants would be met with ex-communication and that they were under the Church’s protection.

Consequently, the movement helped in the reduction of destruction and violence caused by warfare during this time period.

Aside from that, notably, Popes also acted as mediators by negotiating treaties and alliances in conflicts between European states. They also used their spiritual authority to influence the actions of European rulers.

One such example is when the German king Henry IV was [excommunicated](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=448cb4499196755eJmltdHM9MTY3MzkxMzYwMCZpZ3VpZD0yMjEzOGMxNi1kOTM5LTY3NDUtMWRmOC05ZTVlZDg2MzY2MzAmaW5zaWQ9NTIwNQ&ptn=3&hsh=3&fclid=22138c16-d939-6745-1df8-9e5ed8636630&psq=who+excommunicated+the+German+king+Henry+IV+in+1076&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZW5jeWNsb3BlZGlhLmNvbS9oaXN0b3J5L2VuY3ljbG9wZWRpYXMtYWxtYW5hY3MtdHJhbnNjcmlwdHMtYW5kLW1hcHMvZW1wZXJvci1oZW5yeS1pdi1hbmQtcG9wZS1ncmVnb3J5LXZpaQ&ntb=1) by Pope Gregory VII in 1076, causing a substantial weakening of the king’s power.

**Renaissance and Enlightenment Diplomacy**
-------------------------------------------

Diplomacy underwent significant changes during the Renaissance and Enlightenment periods. Along these lines, one of the most important developments of this time period was the establishment of permanent diplomatic missions.

The Renaissance, which began in the 14th century and lasted until the 17th century, marked a cultural and intellectual revolution in Europe.

![Image 6: Diplomacy in International Relations](https://thediplomaticinsight.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/07/diplomacy-1-1024x500.jpg)

While the Enlightenment, which began in the 17th century and lasted until the late 18th century, was a period of intellectual and cultural change marked by a focus on reason and scientific thinking.

Furthermore, the period also saw the emergence of more formalized diplomacy. Diplomatic corps, diplomatic immunity, and protocols for diplomatic communications were established and implemented.

Diplomatic representatives were only sent on a temporary basis for specific negotiations or missions before this era.

However, during the Renaissance and Enlightenment, nations started to establish permanent diplomatic missions in other countries.

As a result, more consistent and efficient communication between states was established which helped to establish a stable system of international relations.

Likewise, the creation of international laws and protocols also played a crucial role in the development of diplomacy during this period.

One such example is the [Congress of Westphalia in 1648](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=57178cbc4a25a394JmltdHM9MTY3MzkxMzYwMCZpZ3VpZD0yMjEzOGMxNi1kOTM5LTY3NDUtMWRmOC05ZTVlZDg2MzY2MzAmaW5zaWQ9NTE3Mg&ptn=3&hsh=3&fclid=22138c16-d939-6745-1df8-9e5ed8636630&psq=Congress+of+Westphalia+in+1648&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYnJpdGFubmljYS5jb20vZXZlbnQvUGVhY2Utb2YtV2VzdHBoYWxpYQ&ntb=1), which established the idea of a nation-state as a legal entity and the principle of sovereignty laying the foundation for the modern system of international diplomacy and law.

Moreover, the [Treaty of Utrecht in 1713](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=86c382a0deae606aJmltdHM9MTY3MzkxMzYwMCZpZ3VpZD0yMjEzOGMxNi1kOTM5LTY3NDUtMWRmOC05ZTVlZDg2MzY2MzAmaW5zaWQ9NTQyNw&ptn=3&hsh=3&fclid=22138c16-d939-6745-1df8-9e5ed8636630&psq=Treaty+of+Utrecht+in+1713&&ntb=1) at the end of the War of the Spanish Succession established the principle of non-interference in the internal affairs of other states.

It is still recognized and is one of the basic principles of modern diplomacy. The Enlightenment also brought with it an emphasis on reason and rationality in diplomacy.

Diplomatic negotiations were no longer based solely on the impulses of monarchs or religious leaders but on a more rational and logical approach.

This emphasis on reason led to the creation of the principle of the balance of power, which holds that the international system is maintained by the balancing of the power of different states.

Also, the establishment of international organizations like the [Congress of Vienna](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=fa83a1afcc0a3243JmltdHM9MTY3MzkxMzYwMCZpZ3VpZD0yMjEzOGMxNi1kOTM5LTY3NDUtMWRmOC05ZTVlZDg2MzY2MzAmaW5zaWQ9NTIxNg&ptn=3&hsh=3&fclid=22138c16-d939-6745-1df8-9e5ed8636630&psq=Congress+of+Vienna&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYnJpdGFubmljYS5jb20vZXZlbnQvQ29uZ3Jlc3Mtb2YtVmllbm5h&ntb=1) in 1815 sought to uphold peace and stability in Europe.

This was another significant development during this time. A system of alliances and treaties was established by this Congress, aiding in the long-term prevention of major wars.

**19th & 20th Century Diplomacy**
---------------------------------

Diplomacy was significantly impacted in the 19th and 20th centuries as a result of major global events; the expansion of colonialism and the two World Wars.

The competition between European powers for territories in Africa known as the [Scramble for Africa](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=716e4daaca75c28bJmltdHM9MTY3MzkxMzYwMCZpZ3VpZD0yMjEzOGMxNi1kOTM5LTY3NDUtMWRmOC05ZTVlZDg2MzY2MzAmaW5zaWQ9NTIxNg&ptn=3&hsh=3&fclid=22138c16-d939-6745-1df8-9e5ed8636630&psq=Scramble+for+Africa&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud29ybGRhdGxhcy5jb20vd2hhdC13YXMtdGhlLXNjcmFtYmxlLWZvci1hZnJpY2EuaHRtbA&ntb=1) was one of the major events that characterized the colonial expansion in the 19th century.

![Image 7: Diplomacy](https://thediplomaticinsight.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/Capture.png)

Diplomacy

This expansion led to increased tensions and competition between colonial powers, resulting in conflicts and wars. Diplomacy during this period was often focused on maintaining and securing colonies, rather than promoting peace and cooperation.

The outbreak of [World War I](https://www.britannica.com/event/World-War-I) in 1914 marked a turning point in the history of diplomacy as it led to the collapse of several empires and the creation of new nation-states.

During the war, diplomacy was focused on negotiating peace treaties and securing alliances. For example, in 1919 the Treaty of Versailles was signed marking the end of the war and establishing a new international order.

However, the outbreak of [World War II](https://www.britannica.com/event/World-War-II) in 1939 had an even greater impact on diplomacy. This led to the emergence of the United States (US) and the Soviet Union as superpowers.

Ergo, diplomacy during the war focused on forming alliances and negotiating peace. Regardless, in this vein, the formation of the [United Nations](https://www.un.org/en/) (UN) in 1945 marked a shift towards multilateral diplomacy.

The UN was established as a forum for nations to resolve and discuss issues through collective action as well as negotiation.

It has played a significant role in maintaining peace and stability in the international community along with promoting multilateral cooperation.

Besides, the UN also designed specialized agencies to address specific global issues. for example the World Health Organization (WHO) and the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO).

In short, the proliferation of colonialism and the two World Wars significantly influenced diplomacy in the 19th and 20th centuries.

While the formation of the UN marked a shift towards multilateral diplomacy, with a focus on resolving conflicts via negotiation.

**Modern Diplomacy**
--------------------

Modern diplomacy encompasses a wide range of activities, including representation, the maintenance of relations, negotiation, and the protection and promotion of national interests.

It also encompasses a variety of actors, including governments, non-state actors, and international organizations.

In comparison to traditional diplomacy which focuses on the relations between monarchs and ambassadors, it focuses on a wider range of issues such as economic, humanitarian, environmental, and technological issues.

# Ancient diplomacy

### See also

Ancient diplomacy refers to the diplomatic techniques and methods employed by ancient civilizations to uphold their relationships with other states or city-states.

In the past, diplomacy frequently comprised conversations between kings, ambassadors, or other envoys from other kingdoms or empires in an effort to forge alliances, resolve conflicts, or establish up trade agreements. The formation of diplomatic missions or embassies in other countries as well as the exchange of gifts, letters, or emissaries were common methods used to carry out these diplomatic activities.

In addition to using military force, ancient diplomacy also encompassed the exploitation of social, economic, and cultural ties. The elaborate diplomatic protocols and traditions served as the foundation for contemporary diplomatic activities. The political, economic, and cultural environment of the ancient world was significantly shaped by ancient diplomacy

--- SOURCE 5: Diplomacy in the ancient Near East - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East

SUMMARY:
The greater ancient Near East (including Egypt) offers some of the oldest evidence of the existence of international relations, since it was there that states "State (polity)") first developed (the city-states and empires of Mesopotamia, the Levant, and Egypt) around the 4th millennium B.C.E. Almost 3000 years of the evolution of diplomatic relations are thus visible in sources from the ancient Near East. The different practices utilized in diplomatic relations in the ancient Near East are essentially known through the royal archives of the 2nd millennium, coming largely from four archaeological sites (Mari, Hattusa, Tell el-Amarna, and Ugarit), complemented by less numerous sources from the preceding millennium (Lagash, Ebla) and the following (Nineveh, Hebrew Bible).

The greater [ancient Near East](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Near_East "Ancient Near East") (including [Egypt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Egypt "Egypt")) offers some of the oldest evidence of the existence of [international relations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_relations "International relations"), since it was there that [states](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") first developed (the [city-states](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/City-state "City-state") and [empires](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Empire "Empire") of [Mesopotamia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), the [Levant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Levant "Levant"), and Egypt) around the 4th millennium B.C.E. Almost 3000 years of the evolution of diplomatic relations are thus visible in sources from the ancient Near East. However, because only certain periods are well documented within that timespan, there remain many gaps in the modern study of diplomacy in this era.

Evolution of diplomatic relations
---------------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Evolution of diplomatic relations")]

The diplomatic relations of the ancient Near East are known only in a fragmented fashion. A limited number of texts allow us to understand relatively well the contemporary diplomatic practices of certain decades, spread out across more than two millennia and large geographical distances. Long periods with little or no documentation are broken up by brief periods of abundant documentation. However, this does not prevent us from understanding the broad threads and general trends in the evolution of international relations, for it follows the evolution of politics itself, which is known in general terms.

### Ancient states period

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ancient states period")]

Diplomatic relations have existed for as long as human communities have been organized into political units, which precedes the period of this study. The first Near Eastern states formed during the 4th millennium B.C.E., but international relations during this era are unknown due to a lack of documentation.

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Foundation_nail_Entemena_Louvre_AO22934.jpg/250px-Foundation_nail_Entemena_Louvre_AO22934.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Foundation_nail_Entemena_Louvre_AO22934.jpg)

Foundation nail commemorating the peace treaty concluded between Entemena of Lagash and Lugal-kinishe-dudu of Uruk

The first surviving documents on the subject of international relations appear towards the end of the [Early Dynastic period](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Early_Dynastic_Period_(Mesopotamia) "Early Dynastic Period (Mesopotamia)") of ancient Mesopotamia (2600-2340 B.C.E.).[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East#cite_note-1) They relate to the [Sumerian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") city-state of [Lagash](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lagash "Lagash"). Among them is the oldest known [treaty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Treaty "Treaty"), concluded between the king of Lagash ([En-metena](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entemena "Entemena")) and the king of [Uruk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uruk "Uruk"). However, the richest of the records concern the series of conflicts between Lagash and its neighbor [Umma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Umma "Umma") between 2600 and 2350 B.C.E.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East#cite_note-2) The documents about these wars are primarily about the military aspects of the war, with little about the diplomatic side. They display the constant rivalries between the cities of southern Mesopotamia. These also appear in the epic stories of the kings of Uruk ([Lugalbanda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lugalbanda "Lugalbanda"), [Enmerkar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enmerkar "Enmerkar"), and [Gilgamesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gilgamesh "Gilgamesh")), which may describe real events. They show the antagonisms pushing Lagash against its rivals, [Kish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kish_(Sumer) "Kish (Sumer)") and [Aratta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aratta "Aratta"). The city of Kish seemed to assert a hegemonic position at certain points— its king, [Mesalim](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mesilim "Mesilim"), intervened around 2600 to arbitrate in the conflict between Lagash and Umma, and the title of "King of Kish" was also assumed by kings of other cities as a sign of superiority.

The site which offers the most important diplomatic archives from this period are those from [Ebla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ebla "Ebla"), in [Syria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syria "Syria").[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East#cite_note-3) The kings of Ebla had contacts with neighboring kings, notably the powerful sovereigns of [Mari](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mari,_Syria "Mari, Syria") and [Nagar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tell_Brak "Tell Brak"), but also with the kings of more distant regions, like Kish in Mesopotamia and [Hamazi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hamazi "Hamazi") in western [Iran](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iran "Iran"). Excavations at Ebla have uncovered the oldest example of a written peace treaty, the [Treaty between Ebla and Abarsal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Treaty_between_Ebla_and_Abarsal "Treaty between Ebla and Abarsal"), as well as proof of matrimonial alliances between the local kings and certain of their allies.

### The first empires

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The first empires")]

[![Image 6](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Alliance_Naram-Sin_Awan_Louvre_Sb8833.jpg/250px-Alliance_Naram-Sin_Awan_Louvre_Sb8833.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Alliance_Naram-Sin_Awan_Louvre_Sb8833.jpg)

Treaty between [Naram-Sin of Akkad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Naram-Sin_of_Akkad "Naram-Sin of Akkad") and a sovereign of Awan, perhaps Khita, c. 2250 B.C.E., Musée de Louvre

Gradually, certain states in southern Mesopotamia were able to exercise hegemony over their neighbors for longer and longer, like Uruk under [Enshakushana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enshakushanna "Enshakushanna") and above all Umma under [Lugal-zagesi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lugal-zage-si "Lugal-zage-si"). This evolution was completed by [Sargon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sargon_of_Akkad "Sargon of Akkad") of [Akkad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Akkad_(city) "Akkad (city)"), who founded the [first empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Akkadian_Empire "Akkadian Empire") in 2340, comprising all of the Mesopotamian city-states, up to those of eastern Syria. The diplomatic relations of this period are not well known. There is a tablet which is inscribed with a peace treaty concluded between [Naram-Sin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Naram-Sin_of_Akkad "Naram-Sin of Akkad") of Akkad (Sargon's grandson) and the king of [Awan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Awan_dynasty "Awan dynasty"), in southwest Iran, who was his [vassal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vassal "Vassal").[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East#cite_note-4) This complete hegemony endured until the second half of the 22nd century B.C.E., when the Akkadian Empire collapsed. This vacuum was filled several decades later by the kings of the [Third Dynasty of Ur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Third_Dynasty_of_Ur "Third Dynasty of Ur"), who founded in their turn the [Neo-Sumerian Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neo-Sumerian_Empire "Neo-Sumerian Empire"). These sovereigns were in communication with their neighboring kingdoms, and they regularly received embassies, and sent their own messengers.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East#cite_note-5) Their diplomatic service was controlled by a dignitary named SUKKAL.MAH. They combined their military expeditions onto the Iranian plateau with matrimonial politics, marrying their daughters to the kings of the regions ([Anshan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anshan_(Persia) "Anshan (Persia)"), Zabshali) in order to win their loyalty and solidify their own legitimacy. However, their empire ended in disintegration, and began a long period without a hegemonic empire, under the [Amorite](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amorites "Amorites") dynasties.

### The second millennium: the balance of powers

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The second millennium: the balance of powers")]

[![Image 7](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Amurru.JPG/250px-Amurru.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Amurru.JPG)

The Near East in the Old Babylonian epoch

The 2nd millennium was a period during which a relative [power equilibrium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balance_of_power_(international_relations) "Balance of power (international relations)") was reached— no kingdom was able to exert power over its neighbors in a durable fashion. This allowed the gradual formation of more and more powerful and stable states, which dominated the international stage, and controlled a certain number of vassals, which tended to reduce over time. These vassals were the object of many rivalries between the dominant kingdoms, which sometimes resulted in overt conflict.

[![Image 8](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Kadesh.jpg/250px-Kadesh.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Kadesh.jpg)

Copy of the Hittite version of the [Egyptian–Hittite peace treaty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Egyptian%E2%80%93Hittite_peace_treaty "Egyptian–Hittite peace treaty") between [Ḫattušili III](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E1%B8%AAattu%C5%A1ili_III "Ḫattušili III") and [Ramesses II](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ramesses_II "Ramesses II"), written in [Akkadian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Akkadian_language "Akkadian language") and found at [Hattusa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hattusa "Hattusa") ([Istanbul Archaeology Museums](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Istanbul_Archaeology_Museums "Istanbul Archaeology Museums"))

The first half of the millennium was the era of the Amorite Kingdoms (2004-1595 B.C.E.), which formed a kind of _koiné_ of political practices, similar from the Mediterranean to the Zagros foothills.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East#cite_note-6) Mesopotamia and Syria were dominated successively by many kingdoms— first by [Isin and Larsa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isin-Larsa_period "Isin-Larsa period"), who took the succession of Ur, but finally by [Babylon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First_Babylonian_dynasty "First Babylonian dynasty"), which imposed its hegemony under [Hammurabi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hammurabi "Hammurabi") (1792–1750) and his successors, just as [Elam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elam "Elam") failed to impose its power over Mesopotamia. In Syria, the dominant kingdom was that of [Yamkhad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yamhad "Yamhad") ([Aleppo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aleppo "Aleppo")), which profited from the dissipation of its neighbors Mari and [Qatna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qatna "Qatna") (its greatest rival) over the course of the 18th and 17th centuries. The diplomatic practices of this era were documented mainly by the exceptional royal archives of [Mari](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mari,_Syria "Mari, Syria"), dating from the first half of the 18th century (diplomatic correspondence, political accords, historical records), but also supplemented by the more minor archives of [Tell Leilan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tell_Leilan "Tell Leilan"), [Tell Rimah](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tell_al-Rimah "Tell al-Rimah"), [Kültepe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/K%C3%BCltepe "Kültepe"), and others.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East#cite_note-7)

The end of the Amorite period was marked by the destruction of its two great kingdoms by the [Hittites](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hittites "Hittites"), who had reunited the kingdoms of eastern [Anatolia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia") in the last decades of the 17th century. At the same time, the [Hurrians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hurrians "Hurrians") began to found increasingly powerful political entities, culminating in the formation of the kingdom of [Mitanni](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mitanni "Mitanni"). To these two were added Egypt. After the 18th dynasty had driven out the Hyksos, they invaded several decades later the Levant, expanding their empire their, bordering Mitanni. This marked a new era, with larger, more powerful, and more culturally diverse kingdoms.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East#cite_note-8) Most of the ancient Amorite kingdoms became vassals of the new rulers, with the exception of Babylon, which remained an important power under the Kassites (1595–1155). The sovereigns of these dominant kingdoms were considered as "great kings", equal among each other. Beginning in the 14th century, Assyria replaced Mitanni in this group. Elam could also be considered one of the great kingdoms of this period, at least in the 13th and 12th centuries. The diplomatic practices of this period are known thanks to several collections of exceptional importance: those of Hattusa, the Hittite capital (letters, diplomatic accords, historical chronicles), the letters of Amarna, in Egypt (international correspondence of the pharaohs Amenhotep III and Akhenaton), and the royal archives of Ugarit, a minor Syrian kingdom controlled first by Egypt and later by the Hittites.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East#cite_note-9) It is during this period that international relations are documented by the most abundant and geographically diverse sources.

[![Image 9: 14th century B.C.E.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Moyen_Orient_Amarna_1.svg/500px-Moyen_Orient_Amarna_1.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Moyen_Orient_Amarna_1.svg)

[![Image 10: 13th century B.C.E.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Moyen_Orient_13e_si%C3%A8cle.svg/500px-Moyen_Orient_13e_si%C3%A8cle.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Moyen_Orient_13e_si%C3%A8cle.svg)

The situation in the Middle East at the beginning of the period covered by the Amarna Letters. First half of the 14th century (left), and 13th century after the expansion of the Hittites and Assyrians (right).

### The empires of the first millennium

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: The empires of the first millennium")]

[![Image 11](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Jehu-on-black-obelisk.jpg/250px-Jehu-on-black-obelisk.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Jehu-on-black-obelisk.jpg)

 Bas-relief showing King [Jehu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jehu "Jehu") of [Judea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Judea "Judea") prostrating himself before the Assyrian king [Shalmaneser III](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shalmaneser_III "Shalmaneser III"), with the list of tributes given listed below.

The period of the "great kings" ended around 1200, with the arrival of great political changes, notably the successive migrations of the Sea Peoples and the Arameans, the collapse of the dominant kingdoms' power, and the creation of many smaller kingdoms in Syria, the Levant, and Anatolia, which were not under the control of any larger power. This transformation is known as the [Late Bronze Age Collapse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Late_Bronze_Age_collapse "Late Bronze Age collapse"). The Books of the Prophets of the Hebrew Bible allow us to observe the relations of two of these states (Israel and Judea) with their neighbors.

Assyria was the only one of these great kingdoms to preserve its power and political stability enough to rebuild [an empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neo-Assyrian_Empire "Neo-Assyrian Empire") starting at the end of the 10th century.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East#cite_note-10) In a little more than two centuries, Assyria imposed its control over most of the Near Eastern kingdoms around it. Only Babylon, Urartu, Elam, and Egypt were able to resist their domination, but they were eventually conquered as well. This period marked the beginning of great hegemonic empires in the Middle East. Because of this, Assyria did not seek to develop very strong diplomatic relations, opting instead to dominate its neighbors. Its relations with other kingdoms were mostly unequal. The foreign relations of the Assyrians, above all those with their vassals, are known from the archives of the Assyrian capitals (mainly Nineveh), complemented by sources from outside the core of the empire (notably the Hebrew Bible).

Between 614 and 609, Assyria was destroyed by an alliance between the Babylonians and the Medes, but the empire of the former did not last even a full century, since their real power did not compare with that of the Medes— the Achaemenid Persians. Under the leadership of Cyrus II, Cambyses, and Darius, they created a lasting empire, which outlasted the Assyrian and Babylonian empires. They did not tolerate any kingdom which purported to be their equal. This empire fell to the invasion of Alexander the Great between 333 and 330, who proved incapable of utilizing the Achaemenid heritage to his advantage, and at his death in 323 his generals divided up the empire. This was the beginning of the Hellenistic period, during which the Mediterranean and the Middle East were divided between several rival great kingdoms.

Diplomatic practices
--------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Diplomatic practices")]

The different practices utilized in diplomatic relations in the ancient Near East are essentially known through the royal archives of the 2nd millennium, coming largely from four archaeological sites (Mari, Hattusa, Tell el-Amarna, and Ugarit), complemented by less numerous sources from the preceding millennium (Lagash, Ebla) and the following (Nineveh, Hebrew Bible). There seems to be a relative homogeneity and continuity in practices. However, this article mainly concerns the 2nd millennium, because of the available sources and because the international context at that time was more conducive to developments in international relations.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East#cite_note-11)

### One big family

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Diplomacy_in_the_ancient_Near_East&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: One big family")]

The kings of the 2nd millennium resembled one large family. The suzerains were the "fathers" (In Akkadian, _abu(m))_ of their vassals, who were called their "sons" (_māru(m)_). Between sovereigns of equal rank (great kings, or else vassals of the same king or of a king of equal rank), rulers were considered "brothers" (_ahu(m)_). enriched_points.expanded_content: Ancient civilizations like Egypt and Mesopotamia utilized treaties and matrimonial alliances to secure peace and strengthen relationships, essential for their survival and prosperity.
The medieval period saw the rise of structured diplomacy, with envoys negotiating treaties and marriages, enhancing political stability and economic relations between states.
The Catholic Church played a significant role in mediating conflicts during the Middle Ages, leveraging its authority to broker peace treaties and influence European rulers.
The Renaissance and Enlightenment periods established permanent diplomatic missions, formalized protocols, and emphasized rational diplomacy, shaping modern international relations and laws.
